1: ἀπόστολος (apostolos) carries the legal sense of a commissioned emissary — not self-appointed but sent with delegated authority. The phrase διὰ θελήματος θεοῦ (by the will of God) is Paul's standard epistolary marker of that commission's source. ἅγιοι (hagioi, saints) is the corporate designation of the people set apart — the whole community, not an elite. Pairing it with πιστοί (pistoi, faithful) indicates both status (set apart) and disposition (trusting).
2: The pairing χάρις καὶ εἰρήνη (grace and peace) is not merely a formal greeting; it fuses the Greek charis (favor bestowed) with the Hebrew shalom (wholeness/well-being), condensing the letter's entire argument into the salutation. The double source — ἀπὸ θεοῦ πατρός... καὶ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — places God and Christ in coordinate origin, a christological weight that the greeting form quietly carries.
3: The eulogy opens with a deliberate Greek wordplay: εὐλογητός (praised/blessed be) and εὐλογήσας (who blessed us) — the same root applied first to God and then to his act toward us. ἐπουρανίοις (epouraniois, heavenly realms/places) is a distinctive Ephesian term appearing five times; it designates not a remote location but the sphere of spiritual reality that interpenetrates the present age. πνευματικῇ εὐλογίᾳ (spiritual blessing) is comprehensive — every spiritual blessing, not a selection.
4: ἐξελέξατο (aorist middle) marks election as a single past act: God chose, at a definite moment (before creation), not as an ongoing process. πρὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου (before the foundation of the world) emphasizes pre-temporal divine initiative; the choice precedes existence. ἁγίους καὶ ἀμώμους (holy and blameless) echoes the sacrificial vocabulary of Leviticus 1:3 — the language of the unblemished offering, now applied to those chosen in Christ.
5: προορίσας (aorist participle) continues the pre-temporal divine purpose: having predestined. υἱοθεσία (huiothesia, adoption as sons/sonship) is a Pauline term drawn from Roman legal practice — formal adoption conferring full legal status as heir. εὐδοκίαν τοῦ θελήματος αὐτοῦ (the good pleasure of his will) underlines that the motive is internal to God — not external compulsion or human merit but his own gracious inclination.
6: The phrase ἐν τῷ ἠγαπημένῳ (in the Beloved) is a title — the perfect passive participle used as a proper designation for Christ, parallel to the Father's declaration at the baptism. The wordplay χάριτος... ἐχαρίτωσεν (grace... graced/freely bestowed) is almost untranslatable: he has graced us with grace. The praise orientation — εἰς ἔπαινον δόξης — frames all of Ephesians 1:3-14 as a doxological movement, not an abstract theological system.
7: ἀπολύτρωσιν (apolutrōsin, redemption) is the technical term for ransoming a slave or prisoner. The phrase διὰ τοῦ αἵματος αὐτοῦ (through his blood) specifies the redemptive price paid. παραπτώματα (transgressions) refers to deliberate sideways steps from the path — distinct from ἁμαρτία (missing the mark); Paul's usage here stresses volitional departure. Both occur together in Ephesians 2:1, suggesting he treats them as overlapping categories of human failure.
8: περισσεύσας (aorist — he lavished/caused to abound) marks a decisive abundance; God did not dispense grace stingily. σοφία καὶ φρόνησις (wisdom and understanding) — the first is the broad capacity for right judgment, the second the practical intelligence to apply it. The lavishing of grace is accompanied by cognitive illumination, not mere forgiveness in isolation.
9: μυστήριον (mystērion) does not mean something permanently unknowable; in Paul it is a divine secret formerly hidden but now disclosed — the word is about revelation, not concealment. κατὰ τὴν εὐδοκίαν αὐτοῦ (according to his good pleasure) again anchors the disclosure in God's internal disposition rather than external pressure. προέθετο (purposed/set forth — aorist middle) suggests a plan laid out in advance and now being executed.
10: ἀνακεφαλαιώσασθαι (anakephalaiōsasthai) is a compound from kephalē (head) — to recapitulate, to sum up under a single head. The word is used in rhetoric for gathering arguments under a thesis; here it describes the eschatological gathering of all things under Christ as head. οἰκονομία (administration/stewardship) frames salvation-history as a managed household economy with appointed times. πλήρωμα τῶν καιρῶν (fullness of the times/seasons) — not chronos (clock time) but kairos (appointed moment).
11: ἐκληρώθημεν (we were allotted/have obtained an inheritance — passive) reverses the image: not only do we inherit but God allots us as his portion, echoing Deuteronomy 32:9 where Israel is the LORD's allotted inheritance. βουλὴ τοῦ θελήματος (counsel of his will) — the deliberative purpose behind all things. κατ᾽ ἐνέργειαν (according to the working) connects purpose to effective operation.
12: τοὺς προηλπικότας (perfect active participle — those who had already hoped) likely distinguishes Jewish believers ("we") who had awaited the Messiah from Gentile believers addressed as "you" in Ephesians 1:13. The perfect tense signals a prior, settled hope. εἰς ἔπαινον δόξης αὐτοῦ — the third occurrence of this doxological refrain in Ephesians 1:6,12,14; the threefold repetition structures the entire passage as praise-oriented.
13: ἐσφραγίσθητε (you were sealed — aorist passive) marks a decisive past event at conversion. The seal in antiquity marked ownership and authenticity; the Holy Spirit is both the agent and the content of that sealing. τῷ πνεύματι τῆς ἐπαγγελίας (the Spirit of the promise — objective genitive) identifies the Spirit as the promised gift long anticipated (Joel 2:28; Isaiah 44:3). The sequence — heard, believed, sealed — is narrative, not three stages requiring separation in time.
14: ἀρραβών (arrhabōn, earnest/deposit) is a commercial term for a down-payment that obligates the giver to pay the remainder. The Spirit is not the whole inheritance but the legally binding pledge of it. ἀπολύτρωσιν τῆς περιποιήσεως (redemption of the possession) — the redemption that results in God's full possession of his people; or his people coming into full possession of their inheritance. The ambiguity is productive: both senses are present.
15: ἀκούσας (aorist participle — after I heard) sets the prayer as a response to news received. The report reached Paul in prison; the Ephesians' faith in the Lord Jesus and love toward all the saints is the specific occasion for the intercession that follows. No thanksgiving is performative here — it arises from received report.
16: οὐ παύομαι εὐχαριστῶν (I do not cease giving thanks — present participle) marks ongoing, unbroken gratitude rather than a single act. μνείαν ποιούμενος (making mention — present participle) indicates that the Ephesians are named in Paul's ongoing prayer practice. The two participles together describe the texture of intercessory prayer: ceaseless and specific.
17: πνεῦμα σοφίας καὶ ἀποκαλύψεως (spirit of wisdom and revelation) — the request is for the Spirit's illuminating function, not general wisdom but specifically revelatory wisdom that discloses God. ἐπιγνώσει (epignōsei, full/precise knowledge — from epiginōskō) carries an intensified prefix; it is knowing that reaches its object rather than approximate awareness. The knowing sought is knowing him — personal knowledge, not doctrinal catalogue.
18: πεφωτισμένους τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς τῆς καρδίας (having been enlightened the eyes of your heart — perfect passive participle) — the heart is the faculty of perception and will; Paul asks that its eyes, once enlightened, might see three things: the hope of calling, the riches of inheritance, and the greatness of power (Ephesians 1:18-19). The genitive τῆς καρδίας is idiomatic: the inner eyes of understanding, not literal sight.
19: Paul stacks four Greek power-words: δύναμις (power, inherent capacity), ἐνέργεια (working, efficient activity), κράτος (dominion, ruling strength), ἰσχύς (might, physical force). The accumulation is not redundant; it signals that every mode in which power can be conceived is operative. The phrase τὸ ὑπερβάλλον μέγεθος (surpassing greatness) adds a superlative that overflows any single category.
20: ἐνεργήκεν (perfect — he wrought/has worked) marks the resurrection as a completed act whose effects persist. ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις (in the heavenly realms — same phrase as Ephesians 1:3) places Christ's session in the same sphere as the believers' blessings; the spatial language is about authority-domain, not geography. ἐκ νεκρῶν (out of dead ones — plural) identifies the resurrection as emergence from among the dead, not merely from a state of death.
21: ὑπεράνω πάσης ἀρχῆς καὶ ἐξουσίας (far above all rule and authority) — the hierarchical terms derive from Jewish angelology; Paul lists four: archē, exousia, dynamis, kyriōtēs. Christ's exaltation supersedes every tier of the cosmic hierarchy whether seen (political) or unseen (angelic). The temporal frame — οὐ μόνον ἐν τῷ αἰῶνι τούτῳ ἀλλὰ καὶ ἐν τῷ μέλλοντι — makes the supremacy permanent across both ages.
22: πάντα ὑπέταξεν ὑπὸ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ is a citation of Psalms 8:6 applied to Christ — the subjection of all things under human feet, fulfilled in the exalted Son of Man. κεφαλὴν ὑπὲρ πάντα τῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ (head over all things for/to the church) — the preposition hyper governs two directions simultaneously: he is head over all things and that headship is given for the benefit of the church. The church is not his head but his body — distinct but integral.
23: τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ τὰ πάντα ἐν πᾶσιν πληρουμένου — the participial phrase tou… plēroumenou is ambiguous: middle (he fills all things) or passive (he who is filled). Most interpreters favor the active reading: Christ is the one who fills all in all, and the church as his body is the sphere in which that fullness is expressed. πλήρωμα (plērōma) becomes a key term in Ephesians and Colossians; here it carries the sense of completion — what makes something full.
